title: Authentication & Authorization
subtitle: Login flows, JWT, sessions vs tokens, and OAuth explained

sections.title: The Login Flow
sections.text:
The most common auth flow works like this:

  1. User submits email + password to POST /login
  2. Server hashes the password and compares it against the stored hash
  3. If it matches, the server creates a session or token that proves authentication
  4. The client stores this credential (cookie or localStorage) and sends it with every subsequent request
  5. The server verifies the credential on each protected request

This is called cookie-based (server stores session) or token-based (client stores JWT) authentication.

steps.label: Authenticated!
steps.desc: Client includes credential in every request

sections.caption: The five-step login flow: credentials in, credential out

sections.title: Password Hashing
sections.text:
Never store passwords in plain text. Hash them using a one-way function designed for passwords. Regular hashing algorithms like SHA-256 are too fast — an attacker can try billions of guesses per second. Use bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt — algorithms designed to be slow and memory-hard.

Each password should have a unique salt (random data added before hashing). This prevents rainbow table attacks — even if two users have the same password, their hashes will be different.
sections.caption: Password hashing: salt + slow hash function = secure storage
sections.callout: Use bcrypt with cost factor 12. Higher = slower = more secure. Argon2 is even better but less widely available. Never use MD5 or SHA-256 for passwords.

sections.title: Sessions vs JWT Tokens
sections.text:
Sessions (cookie-based) — Server stores session data in memory or a database. Client only holds a session ID cookie. Easy to revoke (just delete the session). But requires server-side storage and doesn't work well across multiple servers without a shared session store (like Redis).

JWT (JSON Web Token) — All user info is encoded in a signed token that the client stores. The server can verify the token without any database lookup. Stateless and scalable. But tokens are harder to revoke (you need a blocklist) and can grow large.

items: Client holds session ID cookie only
All data encoded in the token
Can grow large with many claims
Must verify signature on every request

sections.caption: Sessions are server-side and revocable; JWT is stateless and scalable
sections.detail: Many modern applications use both: JWT for API authentication (mobile apps, SPAs) and sessions for web apps where server-side revocation is important. The choice depends on your architecture. If you have a single server or sticky sessions, sessions are simpler. If you have many stateless microservices, JWT is more natural. OAuth 2.0 combines both — the authorization server issues tokens that resource servers verify.
